summary:
The Rise of Moscow (c. 1263–1480 AD) was the gradual process by which the Grand Duchy of Moscow transformed from one of the smallest and most obscure principalities of northeastern Russia into the dominant Russian state and eventually the core of the Russian Empire, absorbing its rival principalities (Tver, Ryazan, [[event:3032|Novgorod]]), throwing off Mongol suzerainty, and establishing the autocratic tradition that would define Russian governance for centuries. Moscow's rise was not inevitable — it was a small, geographically central, well-watered territory that became great through a combination of favorable geography, shrewd politics, and the accidents of dynastic survival.

The Moscow princes accumulated advantage through several strategies: cooperation with the [[event:2305|Golden Horde]] as tax-collectors, which earned them the hated but lucrative role of Mongol intermediaries; accumulation of territory through purchase, inheritance, and marriage; support from the Russian Orthodox Church (the metropolitan moved his seat from Vladimir to Moscow in 1326, giving Moscow religious primacy); and the simple demographic luck of having a male heir in each generation when other dynasties died out. The [[event:3034|Battle of Kulikovo]] (1380) was the symbolic turning point — the first Russian military defeat of a Horde army, giving Dmitri Donskoi heroic status — though it took another century of political maneuvering and [[event:2318|Ivan III's]] diplomacy to formally end Mongol authority in 1480. Moscow's success created a particular political culture: centralized, autocratic, deeply suspicious of Western European influences, and shaped by the memory of Mongol subjugation into a psychology that valued security and order over liberty.
